fields.description:
Challenge: Vulenrabiities in OT can always be closed out by patching, often vulenrbaiities have to be managed, and mitigated in other ways, as such - vulenrabiities can be made difficult to exploit, or can only be exploited from within the system zone. 

Impact: Users can get lost in a see of vulenrabiities, users need to be able to focus on new or unmitigated vulnerabilities. 

Solution: Be able to manage asset vulenrabilities, upon detection vulnerbaility should be created as open, users can then manage the status - 

Open

False Detection

Patched

Mitigated

Vulnerbaility information should be visibile along with the status, any false detections should be hidden, with the option to re-open.

fields.description:
## Epic context (OTSECURITY-9274)

Ingest SCD to support asset enrichment.

**What is an SCD file?**

An SCD file, based on the IEC 61850 standard, is a comprehensive substation configuration file that describes all IEDs in a substation, including their communication parameters, logical connections, datasets, and GOOSE/SV configurations.

## Story scope (frontend)

Deliver UI to manage SCD files in the product: listing/table, upload, delete, and apply workflows aligned with backend ingest APIs.

## Child work

Tracked in subtasks for table UI and upload/delete/apply actions.

Phase 1:

* Implement upload, delete, and apply actions in the SCD management flow (OTSECURITY-10374).

These will display at tabs in a modal with each list have the data structure following sequencially components “device-table“, “conn-links“, “conn-sessions“, “device-allow-policy“, “conn-allow-policy“
* Add description “For allow lists, select data to add to baseline when applying to the system“
* With the allow lists, allow user to select each data using checkbox, default is checked for all data
* Submit the preview with “Apply“ button

fields.description:
The **bulk mass-scan approval** feature lets clients approve pending property changes discovered during network scans — either by selecting specific devices or approving everything at once — and then pushes those changes through the existing batch update pipeline.

---

**How it works**

When approving by selection, the client sends a list of items where each item pairs a device ID with its full property payload (name, type, hardware info, OS details, network interfaces, hostname, custom fields, etc.). When approving all at once, the server does the heavy lifting itself — it reads the pending scan data from the database, converts each row into the same property payload shape, and feeds everything into the same runner.

Either way, the updates get chunked and dispatched to device-detection via RabbitMQ, reusing the exact same `synchronizeDataBatchUpdate` pipeline that the manual batch update flow uses. If the same device ID appears more than once in the selection (for example, two scan results for the same device), the payloads are optionally merged before queuing.

**After a successful queue**, the feature cleans up: it deletes the corresponding rows from both `device_mass_scan_item_data` and `device_snmp_mass_scan_item_data`, but only for the devices that were actually enqueued — not everything indiscriminately.

**Concurrency is guarded** using the same `runBatchUpdateProcessStatus` flag as manual batch updates. If a batch update or bulk approval is already running, the request is rejected immediately with `device.still.running.batch.update`.

---

The key design difference from the existing batch update endpoint is that instead of applying **one shared property object across all selected device IDs**, each device gets its **own individual property payload** — which is what makes it suitable for approving heterogeneous scan results where every device discovered something different.

fields.description:
This task involves updating nginx proxy timeout configurations across all mdots components to improve handling of long-running operations.

## Implementation

Updated proxy timeout settings in nginx configuration files for:

* mdots-enterprise
* mdots-sensor
* mdots-site

## Changes

Increased timeout values from 60 seconds to 1 day for:

* proxy_connect_timeout
* proxy_send_timeout
* proxy_read_timeout

This change affects both production scripts and update scripts configurations to ensure consistent timeout handling across deployments.

## Impact

Improves system reliability by preventing premature connection timeouts during extended operations while maintaining security through other nginx safeguards.

fields.description:
Implement passive processing of Zeek `cdp.log` so the sensor can derive **Layer 2 connections** and **asset** for **Cisco gear** from CDP advertisements.

**Cisco Discovery Protocol (CDP)** is a Layer 2 Cisco-proprietary neighbor-discovery protocol. Devices periodically send CDP frames (often to a well-known multicast destination) so adjacent Cisco devices can learn identifiers such as device ID, port ID, platform, software version, management addresses, VLAN/domain data, and capabilities. It is commonly used on switched/access networks for inventory and topology context.

Example from zeek log:

```
#separator \x09
#set_separator  ,
#empty_field    (empty)
#unset_field    -
#path   cdp
#open   2026-04-13-08-17-48
```

fields.description:
## Summary

  Enhanced NLQ SQL generation for vulnerabilities by adding support for detection metadata fields:

* `confidence_score`
* `category`

Note: **Backend team needs to migrate/deploy updated SQL views** so vulnerabilities_view includes:

* confidence_score
* category

## What Changed

**Vulnerability query capability expanded**

1. NLQ can now generate SQL that filters/aggregates by `category` and `confidence_score`.
2. This enables use-cases such as:

    * open/close/mitigated category analytics
    * high-confidence vulnerability filtering
    * confidence-range reporting
    

  Examples of generated SQL now using the new fields:

* How many vulnerabilities are in category open?

    ```sql
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS "Open Vulnerability Count" FROM vulnerabilities_view WHERE category = 'open' LIMIT 100;
    ```
* How many unpatched vulnerabilities have confidence score above 0.8?

    ```sql
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS "Unpatched Vulnerability Count" FROM vulnerabilities_view WHERE vulnerability_status = 'unpatched' AND confidence_score > 0.8 LIMIT 100;
    ```
* List open unpatched vulnerabilities with confidence score between 0.7 and 0.9

    ```sql
    SELECT vulnerability_id, sensor_id, sensor_name, asset_id, asset_name, ipv4, mac, cve_code, cvss_severity, cvss_score, cvss_version, vulnerability_status, category, confidence_score, remediation_pdf_urls FROM vulnerabilities_view WHERE category = 'open' AND vulnerability_status = 'unpatched' AND confidence_score BETWEEN 0.7 AND 0.9 ORDER BY confidence_score DESC NULLS LAST LIMIT 100
    ```

fields.description:
Cisco Discovery Protocol (CDP) is a proprietary Layer 2 network protocol developed by Cisco Systems. It runs on Cisco devices (routers, switches, access points, etc.) and is used to discover information about directly connected Cisco neighbors.

**How it works:** CDP operates at the data link layer (Layer 2), meaning it works independently of the network layer protocol being used (IP, IPX, etc.). Devices periodically broadcast CDP advertisements out all their interfaces, and neighboring devices listen for and store that information.

**What information CDP shares:**

* Device identifiers (hostname)
* Interface/port information
* Hardware platform and model
* Software version (IOS version)
* IP addresses
* VTP domain and VLAN information
* Duplex and MTU settings
* Native VLAN

‌

We need to support this protocol so we can capture such information from Cisco devices.

fields.description:
Support Schneider Electric EcoStruxure Machine Expert discovery protocol. By supporting this protocol, we also need to extract data from it:

```
"hw_part_number": "TM251MESE",
"hardware_id": "101872",
"firmware_version": "V5.2.11.24",
"node_name": "TM251MESE @0080F40CD904",
"ip_address": "192.168.1.85",
"subnet_mask": "255.255.255.0",
"gateway": "192.168.1.1",
"ip_address_alt": "0.0.0.0",
"project_name": "Untitled2",
"project_author": "Neuralyzer",
"uptime_raw_seconds": 1834497
```

Besides, use [Mapping Model/Part Number to Schneider Vendor - UDP/IP protocol (Schneider Discovery protocol) - MetaDefender OT Security - Confluence](https://opswat.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/NAC3/pages/5198414008/Mapping+Model+Part+Number+to+Schneider+Vendor+-+UDP+IP+protocol+Schneider+Discovery+protocol) to lookup part_number to find Vendor and model name. Ttherefore, we can search CVEs for this device!

Refer to [Passive - get detail fingerprint of Schneider PLC (M221, M241, M251, M261) - MetaDefender OT Security - Confluence](https://opswat.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/NAC3/pages/4735992546/Passive+-+get+detail+fingerprint+of+Schneider+PLC+M221+M241+M251+M261)

fields.summary: [DevOps] 1U Hardware have problem when upgrade to 4.0.0

issuetype.description: Bugs track problems or errors.
issuetype.name: Bug

fields.description:
## Impact

* **Affected**: 1U platforms where the OS disk is behind a controller/backplane that requires specific kernel modules and/or firmware to enumerate storage in the minimal migration initramfs environment.
* **Not affected / rarely affected**: VMs and platforms using common controllers that work with the baseline modules (virtio/AHCI/NVMe), where `/dev/sdX` appears immediately.

## Root cause

The migration phase boots a **custom initramfs** (generated by `mdots-update-script.sh`). In that environment:

1. The script **assumed** the “old partition” is always `${TARGET_DISK}${PART_SUFFIX}3` (typically `/dev/sda3`) and attempted to mount it directly.
2. On the 1U platform, the initramfs did **not reliably enumerate the disk/partitions** early enough because:

    * The initramfs only loaded a small hard-coded set of storage modules (`sd_mod`, `ahci`, `libata`, `nvme`, …).
    * Some 1U storage controllers/HBAs/RAID paths require additional **vendor modules** and frequently **firmware blobs** from `/lib/firmware/*` to bring the disk online.
    
3. The result was that `/dev/sda3` **genuinely did not exist** at the moment the mount was attempted, even though it could appear later (after additional probing), leading to the confusing “it exists when I check” observation.

In short: **the initramfs environment was missing controller enablement and the script relied on a fragile device name/partition number.**

## Solution implemented

The fix makes the initramfs phase both **more portable** across controllers and **less dependent on** `/dev/sda3`.

### 1) Bring up storage controller correctly inside initramfs

At initramfs build time (still in the old OS):

* **Capture storage-related loaded modules** from `/proc/modules` and pass them into the initramfs as `EXTRA_MODULES`.
* **Copy storage-related firmware blobs** referenced by those storage modules (`modinfo -F firmware …`) into the initramfs (`$WORK_DIR/lib/firmware/...`).

At initramfs runtime:

* Load `EXTRA_MODULES` early (second-pass module load), so the same storage controller stack used by the running system is present in the migration environment.
* Trigger a **single gentle storage probe** (partition reread and `partx`), without aggressive repeated rescans by default.

### 2) Stop hard-coding `/dev/sda3` as the only source of truth

When `NEED_SHRINK=1` (the “old partition” path):

* The script still tries `${TARGET_DISK}3` briefly.
* If it’s not present, it **auto-discovers the correct backup partition** by scanning ext4 partitions for a marker file:

    * `mdots-migration-data/rootfs.tar.gz`, or
    * `os/mdots-migration-data/rootfs.tar.gz`
    

This means the upgrade succeeds even if:

* device naming changes (`sda` vs `sdb`), or
* the “right” partition is not exactly `3`, or
* `/dev` nodes appear later than expected.

### 3) Reduce console spam / avoid triggering SATA reset loops

Some 1U systems spam `ataX: SATA link down...` during probing. To keep the migration UI usable:

* Lower console loglevel inside initramfs (logs still available via `dmesg`).
* Avoid periodic rescans by default (rescans can trigger link resets on certain backplanes).

‌

fields.description:
# Zeek 6.0 → 8.0 LTS Upgrade: Plugin Compatibility Fixes

Zeek 8.0 ships Spicy 1.14, which enforces stricter validation than Spicy 1.8 (Zeek 6.0). Four neuralyzer plugin repos require source changes to compile.

---

## Part 1: Root Cause Summary

| Repo | Error | Root Cause |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `neuralyzer-zeek-plugin-goose` | `unexpected RESULT` / `unknown ID 'hilti::Charset::UTF8'` | `result` is a reserved keyword in Spicy 1.14. Also uses deprecated `hilti::` namespace. |
| `neuralyzer-zeek-plugin-icsnpp-ethercat` | `cmake_minimum_required VERSION 3.0` rejected | Zeek 8.0 requires CMake >= 3.15. |
| `neuralyzer-zeek-plugin-mms` | `unknown ID 'hilti::Charset'` / `unit variables must be declared at top-level` / `bitfield item name appears in multiple anonymous bitfields` | Three breaking changes: `hilti::` → `spicy::` namespace, `var` inside switch/if no longer allowed, anonymous bitfield item names must be unique within a unit. |
| `neuralyzer-zeek-plugin-sampled-values` | `unknown ID 'hilti::Charset'` / `bitfield item shadows unit field` / JIT `has no member named 'body'` | Namespace change, bitfield shadowing, and conditional/switch fields in a unit don't generate C++ struct members (cannot be accessed through vector elements). |

## Part 2: Exact Changes Per Repo

### 1. `neuralyzer-zeek-plugin-goose`

**File:** `analyzer/goose.spicy`

* Replace all `hilti::Charset` → `spicy::Charset`
* Rename variable `result` → `res` everywhere (declaration, assignments, return)

```
Find/replace across the file:
  hilti::Charset  →  spicy::Charset
  local result    →  local res
  result = result →  res = res
  return result   →  return res
```

### 2. `neuralyzer-zeek-plugin-icsnpp-ethercat`

**File:** `CMakeLists.txt`

* Line 1: change `cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.0)` → `cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.15)`

### 3. `neuralyzer-zeek-plugin-mms`

**File:** `analyzer/asn1.spicy`

In `type ASN1Tag`, rename anonymous bitfield items to avoid shadowing unit fields:

```
Before:                      After:
  constructed: 5;        →     constructed_bit: 5;
  class: 6..7;           →     class_bits: 6..7;
  $$.class               →     $$.class_bits
  $$.constructed          →     $$.constructed_bit
```

**File:** `analyzer/iec61850_mms.spicy`

* Replace all `hilti::Charset` → `spicy::Charset`
* Move all `var` declarations from inside `switch`/`if` blocks to the top of their unit:

    * `CONFIRMED_REQUEST`: move `var identify: bool` to top
    * `CONFIRMED_RESPONSE`: move `var defineNamedVariableList`, `obtainFile`, `fileClose`, `fileRename`, `fileDelete` to top
    

**File:** `analyzer/mms_lib.spicy`

* Replace all `hilti::Charset` → `spicy::Charset`
* Move `var` declarations from switch/if blocks to unit top level:

    * `OBJECT_SCOPE`: move `var vmdspecific`, `var aaspecific` to top
    * `WRITE_RESPONSE`: move `var success` to top
    * `TYPE_DESCRIPTION`: move `var boolean`, `var generalizedtime`, `var objId` to top
    
* Fix two bitfield blocks that shadow unit fields `tag`/`class`/`constructed`:

```
Leading octet bitfield:
  tag: 0..4           →  tag_bits: 0..4
  constructed: 5      →  constructed_bit: 5
  class: 6..7         →  class_bits: 6..7
  $$.tag               →  $$.tag_bits
  $$.class             →  $$.class_bits
  $$.constructed       →  $$.constructed_bit

Subsequent octet bitfield:
  tag: 0..6            →  tag_ext: 0..6
  $$.tag               →  $$.tag_ext
```

* Rename duplicate anonymous bitfield item names. Each anonymous bitfield in the same unit must have unique names. Append a suffix per bitfield:

```
PARA_SUPPORT_OPTIONS:       bit1..bit8  →  bit1_bf1..bit8_bf1, bit1_bf2..bit8_bf2
SERVICE_SUPPORT_OPTIONS:    bit1..bit8  →  bit1_bf1..bit8_bf1 through bit1_bf10..bit5_bf10
ADDITIONAL_SUPPORT_OPTIONS: bit1..bit8  →  bit1_bf1..bit8_bf1, bit1_bf2..bit8_bf2, bit1_bf3..bit7_bf3
```

### 4. `neuralyzer-zeek-plugin-sampled-values`

**File:** `analyzer/asn1.spicy`

* Replace all `hilti::Charset` → `spicy::Charset`
* In `type ASN1Tag`, rename bitfield items (same as mms):

```
  constructed: 5   →  constructed_bit: 5
  class: 6..7      →  class_bits: 6..7
  $$.class          →  $$.class_bits
  $$.constructed    →  $$.constructed_bit
```

* Restructure `ASN1Message` to avoid conditional/switch fields (Spicy 1.14 does not expose them as C++ struct members when accessed through vector elements). Replace the original switch-based body/application_data with:

```
public type ASN1Message = unit(recursive: bool) {
  var application_id: int32;
  var application_data: bytes;
  var body_data: bytes;

  head: ASN1Header;
  raw_data: bytes &size=self.head.len.len;

  on %done {
    if (self.head.tag.class == ASN1Class::Universal) {
      self.body_data = self.raw_data;
    } else {
      self.application_data = self.raw_data;
      self.application_id = cast<int32>(self.head.tag.type_);
    }
  }
};
```

Key: `body_data`, `application_data`, `application_id` are all `var` fields so they are always accessible as C++ struct members.

**File:** `analyzer/sv.spicy`

* Replace the old `body.seq.submessages` access pattern. Parse body messages from `body_data` var:

```
Before:

After:
  savPDU_body: ASN1::ASN1Message(True)[] &parse-from=self.savPDU.front().body_data;

  on savPDU_body {
    for ( element in self.savPDU_body ) {
```

```
#!/bin/bash
```

### Script 3: fix-mms.sh

```
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
REPO="${1:-neuralyzer-zeek-plugin-mms}"
[ -d "$REPO" ] || { echo "ERROR: $REPO not found"; exit 1; }

# --- All files: hilti:: -> spicy:: ---
for f in "$REPO"/analyzer/*.spicy; do
  sed -i 's/hilti::Charset/spicy::Charset/g' "$f"
done

# --- asn1.spicy: bitfield shadowing in ASN1Tag ---
F="$REPO/analyzer/asn1.spicy"
sed -i '/type ASN1Tag/,/^};/{
  s/^\(  *\)constructed: 5;/\1constructed_bit: 5;/
  s/^\(  *\)class: 6\.\.7;/\1class_bits: 6..7;/
  s/\$\$\.class/\$\$.class_bits/g
  s/\$\$\.constructed/\$\$.constructed_bit/g
}' "$F"

# --- mms_lib.spicy: bitfield shadowing in ID unit ---
F="$REPO/analyzer/mms_lib.spicy"

# ID unit: leading octet bitfield
sed -i '/type ID = unit/,/^};/{
  /# Get ASN.1 tag information from leading octet/,/^[[:space:]]*}$/{
    s/^\(\t*\)tag: 0\.\.4;/\1tag_bits: 0..4;/
    s/^\(\t*\)constructed: 5;/\1constructed_bit: 5;/
    s/^\(\t*\)class: 6\.\.7;/\1class_bits: 6..7;/
    s/\$\$\.tag\b/\$\$.tag_bits/g
    s/\$\$\.class\b/\$\$.class_bits/g
    s/\$\$\.constructed\b/\$\$.constructed_bit/g
  }
  /# Get ASN.1 tag information from subsequent octet/,/^[[:space:]]*}$/{
    s/^\(\t*\)tag: 0\.\.6;/\1tag_ext: 0..6;/
    s/\$\$\.tag\b/\$\$.tag_ext/g
  }
}' "$F"

# --- mms_lib.spicy: duplicate anonymous bitfield item names ---
# Uses python for the complex multi-bitfield renaming
python3 - "$F" << 'PYFIX'
import re, sys

with open(sys.argv[1], 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()

def rename_bitfields_in_unit(match):
    """Within a unit block, find anonymous bitfields and make item names unique."""
    text = match.group(0)
    bf_count = [0]

    def rename_bf(m):
        bf_count[0] += 1
        suffix = f"_bf{bf_count[0]}"
        block = m.group(0)
        # Rename bit1..bit8 in the bitfield definition and its body
        for i in range(1, 9):
            block = re.sub(rf'\bbit{i}\b', f'bit{i}{suffix}', block)
        return block

    # Match anonymous bitfields: leading TAB, colon, bitfield(...) { ... } { ... }
    pattern = r'(\t: bitfield\(\d+\)\s*\{[^}]*\}\s*\{[^}]*\})'
    text = re.sub(pattern, rename_bf, text)
    return text

# Apply to PARA_SUPPORT_OPTIONS, SERVICE_SUPPORT_OPTIONS, ADDITIONAL_SUPPORT_OPTIONS
for unit_name in ['PARA_SUPPORT_OPTIONS', 'SERVICE_SUPPORT_OPTIONS', 'ADDITIONAL_SUPPORT_OPTIONS']:
    pattern = rf'(type {unit_name} = unit \{{.*?^\}};)'
    content = re.sub(pattern, rename_bitfields_in_unit, content, flags=re.DOTALL | re.MULTILINE)

# Also handle the named bitfield 'field5' in SERVICE_SUPPORT_OPTIONS
# Replace bit1..bit8 inside field5: bitfield block
def rename_field5(m):
    block = m.group(0)
    for i in range(1, 9):
        block = re.sub(rf'\bbit{i}\b', f'bit{i}_f5', block)
    return block
content = re.sub(r'(field5\s*:\s*bitfield\(\d+\)\s*\{[^}]*\}\s*\{[^}]*\})', rename_field5, content)

with open(sys.argv[1], 'w') as f:
    f.write(content)
PYFIX

# --- mms_lib.spicy + iec61850_mms.spicy: move var from switch/if to unit top ---
# Uses python for structural changes
for spicy_file in "$REPO/analyzer/mms_lib.spicy" "$REPO/analyzer/iec61850_mms.spicy"; do
python3 - "$spicy_file" << 'PYFIX2'
import re, sys

with open(sys.argv[1], 'r') as f:
    lines = f.readlines()

# Find var declarations inside switch/case blocks and move them to unit top
# Strategy: detect "var X: Type" lines that are indented more than unit-level (2+ tabs)
# and are inside a switch/case block

unit_starts = []  # (line_idx, indent_level)
vars_to_move = []  # (var_line_content, unit_start_idx, original_line_idx)

i = 0
while i < len(lines):
    line = lines[i]
    stripped = line.rstrip()

    # Detect unit start
    if re.match(r'^type \w+ = unit', stripped) or re.match(r'^\t*type \w+ = unit', stripped):
        indent = len(line) - len(line.lstrip('\t'))
        unit_starts.append((i, indent))

    # Detect var inside deep nesting (3+ tabs = inside switch/case)
    m = re.match(r'^(\t{3,})(var \w+\s*:.+);', stripped)
    if m and unit_starts:
        tabs = m.group(1)
        if len(tabs) >= 3:  # inside a switch case
            var_decl = m.group(2)
            # Find the enclosing unit
            unit_idx = unit_starts[-1][0]
            vars_to_move.append((var_decl, unit_idx, i))

    i += 1

# Apply changes in reverse order to preserve line numbers
# First, comment out the original var lines (replace with assignment if possible)
for var_decl, unit_idx, orig_idx in reversed(vars_to_move):
    orig_line = lines[orig_idx]
    indent = re.match(r'^(\s*)', orig_line).group(1)

    # Extract var name and check if it has a default value
    vm = re.match(r'var (\w+)\s*:\s*\S+\s*=\s*(.+)', var_decl)
    if vm:
        # Has default value - convert to assignment
        lines[orig_idx] = f"{indent}self.{vm.group(1)} = {vm.group(2)};\n"
    else:
        # No default - just remove (it's a declaration only)
        vm2 = re.match(r'var (\w+)\s*:\s*(\S+)', var_decl)
        if vm2:
            # Check if there's a parse after it
            lines[orig_idx] = f"{indent}# var moved to unit top\n"

# Insert var declarations at unit top (after the opening { line)
inserted = {}
for var_decl, unit_idx, orig_idx in vars_to_move:
    if unit_idx not in inserted:
        inserted[unit_idx] = []
    if var_decl not in inserted[unit_idx]:
        inserted[unit_idx].append(var_decl)

for unit_idx in sorted(inserted.keys(), reverse=True):
    # Find the line with opening brace
    for j in range(unit_idx, min(unit_idx + 5, len(lines))):
        if '{' in lines[j]:
            insert_pos = j + 1
            for var_decl in reversed(inserted[unit_idx]):
                lines.insert(insert_pos, f"\t{var_decl};\n")
            break

with open(sys.argv[1], 'w') as f:
    f.writelines(lines)
PYFIX2
done

echo "OK: $REPO fixed"
```

### Script 4: fix-sampled-values.sh

```
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
REPO="${1:-neuralyzer-zeek-plugin-sampled-values}"
[ -d "$REPO" ] || { echo "ERROR: $REPO not found"; exit 1; }

# --- asn1.spicy ---
F="$REPO/analyzer/asn1.spicy"

# hilti:: -> spicy:: namespace
sed -i 's/hilti::Charset/spicy::Charset/g' "$F"

# Bitfield shadowing in ASN1Tag
sed -i '/type ASN1Tag/,/^};/{
  s/^\(  *\)constructed: 5;/\1constructed_bit: 5;/
  s/^\(  *\)class: 6\.\.7;/\1class_bits: 6..7;/
  s/\$\$\.class/\$\$.class_bits/g
  s/\$\$\.constructed/\$\$.constructed_bit/g
}' "$F"

# Restructure ASN1Message: replace switch/if body with var-based approach
python3 - "$F" << 'PYFIX'
import re, sys

with open(sys.argv[1], 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()

old_pattern = r'public type ASN1Message = unit\(recursive: bool\) \{.*?^\};'
new_block = '''public type ASN1Message = unit(recursive: bool) {
  var application_id: int32;
  var application_data: bytes;
  var body_data: bytes;

  head: ASN1Header;
  raw_data: bytes &size=self.head.len.len;

  on %done {
    if (self.head.tag.class == ASN1Class::Universal) {
      self.body_data = self.raw_data;
    } else {
      self.application_data = self.raw_data;
      self.application_id = cast<int32>(self.head.tag.type_);
    }
  }
};'''

content = re.sub(old_pattern, new_block, content, flags=re.DOTALL | re.MULTILINE)

with open(sys.argv[1], 'w') as f:
    f.write(content)
PYFIX

# --- sv.spicy ---
F="$REPO/analyzer/sv.spicy"

# Replace body.seq.submessages access with var-based approach
sed -i '/savPDU.*front()\.body/d' "$F"
sed -i '/sequence_of_ASDUs/d' "$F"

# Add savPDU_body field and update the hook
sed -i '/savPDU:.*parse-from.*svAPDUdata/a\
\
\tsavPDU_body: ASN1::ASN1Message(True)[] \&parse-from=self.savPDU.front().body_data;' "$F"

sed -i 's/on savPDU {/on savPDU_body {/' "$F"
sed -i 's/for.*element.*in.*self\.savPDU\.front()\.body\.seq\.submessages.*/\t\tfor ( element in self.savPDU_body ) {/' "$F"

echo "OK: $REPO fixed"
```

fields.description:
## Epic context

Parent: [OTSECURITY-8827](https://opswat.atlassian.net/browse/OTSECURITY-8827) — _Bulk Approval for Smart Profiling Changes_

When a smart profile run returns a large number of devices with proposed changes, approving each change one-by-one is onerous. Customers want **bulk** or **auto-acceptance** workflows for smart profiling results.

## Scope of this task

Deliver **batch approve** after **scanning multiple devices** in a smart profiling flow: operators can approve many pending smart-profile changes in one action (instead of only per-row / per-device approval), once scan results are available for multiple targets.

This task covers the **product behavior** (API and/or orchestration as needed) so that batch approval is supported after a multi-device scan. Coordinate with frontend bulk actions on the scan result UI.

## Related work

* Subtask: [OTSECURITY-10382](https://opswat.atlassian.net/browse/OTSECURITY-10382) — _\[FE\] Add Ignore All, Ignore Selected, Accept Selected, Accept All when viewing Smart Profiling scan result_

## Acceptance criteria (draft)

* \[ \] After a smart profiling scan that affects **multiple** devices/rows, the user can trigger **batch approve** (single action) for the applicable pending changes.
* \[ \] Batch approve applies consistently with existing single-approve rules (validation, permissions, and audit/logging expectations).
* \[ \] **Partial failures** are handled: the user gets clear feedback if some items cannot be approved in the batch.
* \[ \] Behavior is aligned with [OTSECURITY-8827](https://opswat.atlassian.net/browse/OTSECURITY-8827) bulk-approval intent and with [OTSECURITY-10382](https://opswat.atlassian.net/browse/OTSECURITY-10382) UI entry points (Accept All / Accept Selected as applicable).

## Notes

* Clarify with PM/UX whether "batch approve" is strictly **Accept all** vs **Accept selected** and how it interacts with ignore/reject flows.
* Document any new or updated APIs for QA and for the FE subtask.

fields.description:
## Ingest SCD to support asset enrichment :   
  
What is an SCD File?

An SCD file, based on the IEC 61850 standard, is a comprehensive substation configuration file that describes all IEDs in a substation, including their communication parameters, logical connections, datasets, and GOOSE/SV configurations. It is generated by a System Configuration Tool (SCT) through the integration of multiple ICD (IED Capability Description) and SSD (System Specification Description) files.

‌

## Benefits of Using SCD Files

* Automated IED Recognition: Upon importing an SCD file, the system automatically identifies each IED’s model, communication parameters, dataset configuration, and more, minimizing manual configuration.
* Rapid Deployment of Logic Relations: The SCD file contains complete GOOSE, MMS, and SV communication logic, which can be deployed across devices with a single import.
* Standardization for Error Prevention: IEC 61850’s standardized data modeling and naming conventions improve consistency and reduce human error.
* Support for Secondary Development and Testing: SCD files can also be used in virtual simulation, system debugging, and automated testing platforms, significantly improving engineering efficiency.

fields.description:
Migrate the legacy Python passive scanning system to Rust for improved performance, reliability, and maintainability while maintaining full functional compatibility.

## Key Architectural Changes

### 1. Threading Model & GIL Elimination

* **Before**: Python GIL serializes 15+ protocol threads on single CPU core
* **After**: Rust true parallelism across multiple CPU cores with async/await
* **Benefit**: Full CPU utilization without Global Interpreter Lock constraints

### 2. Protocol Layer Process Separation

* **Before**: Single Python process handles all 15+ protocols mixed together
* **After**: Separate processes for Special (40% CPU), Layer 2 (20% CPU), Layer 3 (20% CPU), Log Analyzer (10% CPU), Config Sync (5% CPU), and Orchestrator (5% CPU)
* **Benefit**: Protocol layer isolation with dedicated CPU allocation per network analysis type

### 3. CPU Binding & Resource Management

* **Before**: Manual thread management with no CPU affinity control
* **After**: Systemd CPU core allocation via `NNS_CPU_CORES` environment variable
* **Benefit**: Predictable performance and NUMA-aware resource allocation

### 4. Concurrent Task Execution

* **Before**: Thread-based blocking I/O with context switching overhead
* **After**: Async/await non-blocking I/O with work-stealing scheduler
* **Benefit**: Higher throughput and lower latency for protocol analysis

### 5. Protocol Layer Component Isolation

* **Before**: All protocols (ARP, DNS, Modbus, S7Comm, EtherCAT, LLDP, etc.) share global state
* **After**: Layer 2 protocols (ARP, LLDP, EtherCAT, PROFINET) isolated from Layer 3 protocols (DNS, Modbus, S7Comm, TCP connections) with separate CPU allocations
* **Benefit**: Protocol layer crashes don't cascade to other network analysis functions

## Technical Benefits

### Concurrency & Parallelism

* **No GIL**: True parallel execution across CPU cores for protocol analysis
* **Multi-Process**: Independent processes prevent cascade failures
* **CPU Affinity**: Dedicated core assignment eliminates CPU contention
* **Async I/O**: Non-blocking operations prevent thread pool exhaustion

### System Resource Optimization

* **Thread Efficiency**: Work-stealing scheduler maximizes CPU core utilization
* **Memory Management**: Automatic allocation without garbage collection pauses
* **Core Binding**: Systemd controls per-component CPU allocation
* **NUMA Awareness**: Optimized memory access patterns on multi-socket systems

### Operational Reliability

* **Process Isolation**: Component crashes don't affect other passive scanning functions
* **Type Safety**: Compile-time guarantees eliminate entire classes of runtime errors
* **Structured Errors**: `Result<T, E>` pattern prevents silent failures
* **Hot Restart**: Individual components can restart without full system downtime

### Development & Maintenance

* **Thread Safety**: Rust ownership prevents data races at compile time
* **Dependency Injection**: Container architecture enables comprehensive testing
* **Performance Profiling**: Built-in hot path analysis identifies bottlenecks
* **Zero-Cost Abstractions**: High-level code compiles to efficient native code

fields.description:
MD-OTS was built on a legacy OPSWAT Linux platform which is heavy weight, not streamlined and optimized for MD OTS. 

Impact : slow performance and heavy compute requirements. This also prevents effective deployment on IFW as a sensor and prevents native AMI deployment. 

Solution : Develop MD OTS Distro that is optimized for MD OTS. More info is here [MD OT Security Distro - MetaDefender OT Security - Confluence](https://opswat.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/NAC3/pages/4767810050/MD+OT+Security+Distro)
